text: The colloanal sleeve operation is a workhorse to get out of trouble in the pelvis. If you have a stapler misfire low in the pelvis, then converting this to an endoanal colloanal anastomosis can get you out of trouble. When there is a patient who has a previously failed anastomosis with a pelvic abscess, pelvis is hostile, and sometimes the safest place to be is within the intestinal lumen. The pull-through operation can be used in those circumstances.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and dissects the rectum down to the point of maximum difficulty, dividing it at that level. A mucozectomy is performed from above and below to the dentate line. The splenic flexure is mobilized, and the colon is passed through the mucosectomized rectum to the anal canal, where it is hand-anastomosed. A loop ileostomy is constructed at the end.

The patient is a seventy-eight-year-old man who in the past had had a radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer. Subsequently, he developed a rectal cancer for which he received chemoradiation and then a low anterior resection. Regrettably, he then developed a fistula from the anastomosis to the previous radical prostatectomy site as a rectourethral fistula. The planned operation is Suave's colloanal pull-through anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a full-length laparotomy, mobilizing the splenic flexure. The colon is observed running along the marginal artery, with ample reach in the left colon. The procedure progresses deep into the pelvis, reaching the point of maximum difficulty.
A budget-friendly version of the Lone Star retractor is used to expose the surgical field, clearly demonstrating the fistula connecting to the prostatic cavity.

The surgeon injects a one in two hundred thousand dilute adrenaline solution to raise a mucosal bleb. Scissors are used to disconnect the dentate line, initiating the mucozectomy. The procedure carefully preserves the underlying internal sphincter while extending the mucozectomy up to the divided rectal segment.
Strips of mucosa are being removed from the rectal segment, demonstrating the ease of operation when the distal mucosectomy is kept short.

The surgeon curettes the fistula track and cavity, then delivers the colon through the anus. The orientation of the colon is confirmed, and the lumen is opened, ensuring there is no twist in the mesentery.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis with the colon hanging outside the anal canal, making the placement of the first four sutures awkward. The prolapsing colon is then reduced, and the sutures are tied, resulting in a tension-free anastomosis.

The Eisenhammer retractor is positioned within the anal canal, allowing visualization of the surgical field at various clock positions. The surgeon reverses and rotates the suture needle to facilitate transanal suturing, with an assistant assisting by grabbing the needle tip and passing it kephalad before withdrawing it from the anus. The anal dilatation is notably reduced, occurring only intermittently when the Eisenhammer retractor is in place, contrasting with the prolonged dilatation seen with the Lone Star retractor.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis site, inserting additional sutures as needed to ensure proper alignment. Stay sutures are removed, allowing the anus to return to its normal configuration.

The following tips are helpful. Perform as much of the rectal dissection abdominally as possible. This is because abdominal mucozectomy is difficult. The safest point in a hazardous pelvis is inside the bowel lumen. Be prepared to split the rectal tube in the midline posteriorly and open it like a book. Hang the colon outside the anus before reducing to ensure lack of tension. Imagine an open and then a closed accordion. If possible, bring down an adjunctive omentoplasty.
The suave coloanal sleeve anastomosis is an essential part of a colorectal surgeon's armamentarium.
